I think that we could be smarter and do that at the batch object level. For instance:
```python
class FacebookAdsApiBatch(object):
    def __init__(self, api, transient_policy=None):
        self._api = api
        ...
        # it's part of a enum like ["SUCCESS", "SILENT", "FAILURE"]
        self.transient_policy = transient_policy
```

*This is my rambling train of thought. I end up thinking I should close this PR unmerged. Jump to "Conclusions" for the results.*

*(This is just to think "out-loud" in public. Perhaps it belongs in a blog post and not here.)*

---------

### Philosophy of "backwards compatible"

Yeah, it's interesting that, in some sense, nothing is really backwards compatible. Any behavior exposed to the user could be relied upon and therefore break when it is changed.

> A bug fix is defined as an internal change that fixes incorrect behavior.

But is there such thing as incorrect behavior if it doesn't manifest as observable behavior? [If a *bug* falls in a forest...](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/If_a_tree_falls_in_a_forest)

------

### It's a trade-off

Anyways, things get messy in both extremes. If you make large changes in "patch" or "minor" versions, things break and users are unhappy.

At the other extreme, if you mostly just use major versions (because everything is a backwards incompatible change), then anyone who depends on your library transitively is going to have ["dependency hell"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dependency_hell) problems.

It's a trade-off between not breaking existing code, and throwing users into dependency hell.

------

### Dependency Hell (example)

Imagine that ciso8601 is at version `2.0.1` (which it is right now).

Say there are 2 libraries that both rely on ciso8601, named "A" and "B". Originally, they both use `>=2,<3` of ciso8601.

Then imagine that there is a project "C" which uses both A and B.

Then version `3.0.0` of ciso8601 is released. It added a bug fix (like the one we've been discussing), and some new independent functionality (so it arguably might have been called `2.1.0` instead).
Library A updates its version of ciso8601 to `>=3,<4` and takes advantage of the new functionality, but library B does not.

Project C wants to make use of the new features of A, but cannot since it still needs B hasn't bumped its version of ciso8601 to `>=3,<4`, and Python cannot run different versions of the ciso8601 at the same time.
pip will install either `3.0.0` or `2.1.0` depending on the order it resolves the dependencies in. If it happens to get the `2.1.0` version, then Library A will fail, since it relies on the new functionality of `3.0.0`.

It gets worse when B is no longer maintained, and will never increment the major version of ciso8601.

Of course, this situation could occur naturally with major, incompatible versions. But if the changes made that caused version `3.0.0` were "largely compatible", then it might have been better if they had been a minor (`2.1.0`) release, because then B could just be run with the new 2.1.0 release. B's behavior will have changed, and there is some chance that this will cause unwanted behavior. But perhaps this is better than wrangling transitive dependencies?

-------

### Conclusions

I guess the conclusion is: As a user of a library, there are no short-cuts from reading the CHANGELOG carefully, and testing each new version. 

**Result 1**: Our CHANGELOG should be carefully made to capture all changes to behavior.

SemVer isn't a great solution, so I shouldn't try to formalize its use in ciso8601. 

**Result 2:** I'll close this PR (unmerged).

We could define our own guidelines for version numbering. Not as a replacement/competitor for SemVer, but just for the project's use. In my mind, it would be something like `major.minor.patch`, where:

* major means "You're going to have to change your code to use this version."
* minor means "We changed internals, or added independent functionality (and possibly did patch changes). You probably don't care."
* patch means "Simple bug fix. you probably don't care"

The goal would be that most users who pin a major version (ex. `>=2,<3`) should find that they never need to change this (until they are ready to change to the next major version).

I think that the result will be largely similar to what SemVer tries to achieve. But since it's all subjective, it's probably best to avoid mentioning SemVer, since SemVer pretends to offer some formal contract to the user (when it actually doesn't).

**Result 3:** Consider writing the above guidelines in a doc somewhere?

The YAML version works too.  I just wanted to introduce the "Current" type here for pedagogical reasons, not because the other way doesn't work any more.  

In particular, it seemed useful to have a couple Current types early in this demo before the ones in the truth catalog section.  This and pixel_scale later are both constants, but the ones in the truth output are not.  That's the main advantage of Current over the YAML `*` notation.  The former gets the current value of things that may change from one galaxy to another.  The `*` notation only works for constants within the YAML file.

I also moved the introduction of `eval_variables` to demo11, since its use is a bit richer in that demo than what we had here.  It had been a fairly lame example in this demo, imo.  But combining it with the YAML tags is a nice design pattern that I've used in practice to put all the constants you might want to modify all together near the top of the config file.

Okay, new plots.  Main caveat to following is this is only on my laptop.

Here's the time for each (n, r) up to n=100.

![unknown](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3650485/37002891-0540673c-2080-11e8-8483-0d4c3686b9ff.png)

So pretty flat for the naive algorithm, and a pretty significant dependence on n and r for the speedy algorithms.

Same thing as ratios:

![unknown-1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3650485/37002948-30633ed0-2080-11e8-90ab-28e44cd11a6e.png)

So the new speedy algorithm is indeed speedier for min(r, n-r) << n/2, but slower nearer the center.

Zooming out:

![unknown-2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3650485/37002987-4edad9e0-2080-11e8-8693-0ac98266f7a2.png)

Naive depends on n, but not so much on r.  Both speedies scale with both r and n.

![unknown-3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3650485/37003021-711b3cf2-2080-11e8-9330-3f04eaf22212.png)

New and old speedy are about the same in the r, n ~= 100s range.  It's also clear here that, as Mike said, the potential relative speed gains are much larger than the potential speed losses comparing speedy vs naive.

That said, the places where speedy is much speedier are speedy already.  I.e., the fractional improvements are large, but the absolute improvements not so much.  If I just add up all the times for all the (n, r) combinations for each algorithm, I get:

n<100
-------
naive: 10.26
speedy_old: 17.06
speedy_new: 19.16

n < 1000
---------
naive: 38.84
speedy_old: 36.99
speedy_new: 37.01

So assuming a uniform prior over (n, r) with n<1000 (reasonable?), it really doesn't make much difference to the expected value of calculation time.  Over n<100, then naive clearly wins.

There's no such thing as read-only schema attributes, only read-only objects.

It looks like the real problem is in `export`ing and `load`ing jobs in the GPI. For example, a job which has been creaded and submitted and is then `export(j)`ed will make a file like:
```python
Job (
   comment = '',
   parallel_submit = False,
   do_auto_resubmit = False,
   name = '',
   inputfiles =[],
   backend =   Local (
     nice = 0
   ),
   application =   Executable (
     exe = 'echo',
     hash = 'cfa689697ba25ae38f7a47b232ea3e7c',
     env = [],
     args = ['Hello World'],
     is_prepared = ShareDir(name='conf-47de7c78-e402-4c59-af7f-29138635fcfa',subdir='.')
   ),
   inputdata =None,
   outputfiles =[],
   postprocessors =   MultiPostProcessor (
   ),
   info =   JobInfo (
     monitor =None
   ),
   splitter =None,
   inputsandbox =[]
 )
```

The problem come when Python is parsing the
```python
Executable (
  exe = 'echo',
  hash = 'cfa689697ba25ae38f7a47b232ea3e7c',
  env = [],
  args = ['Hello World'],
  is_prepared = ShareDir(name='conf-47de7c78-e402-4c59-af7f-29138635fcfa',subdir='.')
)
```
part. Since the order in which the arguments are processed is not fixed, the `is_prepared` attribute is being assigned before e.g. the `exe` attribute. This means that Ganga raises an error saying "This object is in the prepared state so you cannot change it".

The underlying problem here is that we want to ignore the read-only state of an object while it is being constructed and only start enforcing it once its state is fully defined. I'm working on a patch to this PR which will fix this is a slightly different way.

> The race condition was removed when I fixed the toBlock to the latest known

Maybe we are not talking about the same thing, the race I'm referring to cannot be removed, we can not stop the blockchain. It's always possible to query for the current block number just before a new block is mined.

What we can do is have a consistent view of the known latest block inside the node, for that to work we need to have a single place were this update can happen (i.e. the [alarm task](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/6444580a4208a32afcebc5415c4c34db671967c2/raiden/tasks.py#L77-L100)), the rest of the code base should use [that value as the "source of truth"](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/raiden_service.py#L326-L327), and this needs to be protected [by a lock to serialize new updates](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/raiden_service.py#L375).

Note: The existing code *is also* susceptible to races on the filter, which needs to be fixed.
